architecture.summary: The paper applies Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) to steer LLM outputs by decomposing hidden activations into interpretable features. A supervised pipeline selects and labels SAE features, which are then used to intervene on model activations during generation. The seam is the model's own hidden states: SAE features are extracted from internal activations, labeled via supervised methods, and written back through activation steering to modify output behavior.

biblio.title: Steering LLMs? Actually, Sparse Autoencoders can outperform simple baselines

claims.statement: Sparse Autoencoders can perform close to on par with the reference LoRA performance on the AxBench benchmark, when features are selected and labelled with our supervised pipeline.

baseline.name: simple baselines from Wu et al. (2025)

claims.statement: SAEs did not live up to their original hype due to poor steering performance relative to a set of simple baselines in Wu et al. (2025), but this work serves as a partial rebuttal showing SAEs perform closer to reference methods when properly configured.
The supervised pipeline selects features that are surprisingly causal of their identified labels when using only its interpretability-based components.

baseline.name: Wang et al. (2025) findings on sparsity importance

claims.statement: High sparsity (low l0) may not be crucial for successful steering based on interpretability, which is in contrast to the earlier findings in Wang et al. (2025).
